 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">42 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">During 2016 we registered an income tax expense of $31&nbsp;million, while in 2015 and 2014, we
registered an income tax benefit of $21&nbsp;million and $23&nbsp;million, respectively. These amounts reflect the actual taxes calculated on our income before income taxes in each of our jurisdictions. These tax benefits included the recognition of
deferred tax assets, net of valuation allowances, associated with our estimates of the net operating loss recoverability in certain jurisdictions, <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">one-time</FONT> tax benefits related to previous year positions and
our best estimate on additional tax charges related to potential uncertain tax positions and claims. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The 2016 income tax expense included
a <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">one-time</FONT> income of $6&nbsp;million related to the provision reversal following positive settlements of two local tax assessments. The 2015 income tax benefit included a
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">one-time</FONT> income of $46&nbsp;million related to the positive settlement of a local tax assessment, while the year 2014 included a <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">one-time</FONT> income of $35&nbsp;million due
to a favorable interpretation of income tax law related to previous years income tax and to the entering into an advanced pricing agreement with a local revenue service. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our tax rate is variable and depends on changes in the level of operating results within various local jurisdictions and on changes in the
applicable taxation rates of these jurisdictions, as well as changes in estimations of our tax provisions. Our income tax amounts and rates depend also on our loss carry-forwards and their relevant valuation allowances, which are based on estimated
projected plans and available tax planning strategies; in the case of material changes in these plans, the valuation allowances could be adjusted accordingly with an impact on our tax charges. We currently enjoy certain tax benefits in some
countries. Such benefits may not be available in the future due to changes in the local jurisdictions; our effective tax rate could be different in future periods and may increase in the coming&nbsp;years. In addition, our&nbsp;yearly income tax
charges include the estimated impact of provisions related to tax positions which have been considered uncertain. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B><I>Net loss (income) attributable to

compared to $0.12 and $0.14&nbsp;for 2015 and 2014, respectively. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">We also present Adjusted Earnings per Share, which is a non U.S. GAAP
measure. Adjusted Earnings per Share is used to help management and investors understand our operations and to highlight the impact of excluded items like impairment, restructuring charges and other related closure costs and other <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">one-time</FONT> items, net of the estimated relevant tax impact. We believe Adjusted Earnings per Share provides useful information for management and investors because they measure our capacity to generate profits from
our business operations, excluding the expenses related to the rationalizing of our activities and sites that we do not consider to be part of our <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">on-going</FONT> operating results, thereby offering, when read in
conjunction with our U.S. GAAP financials, (i)&nbsp;the ability to make more meaningful <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">period-to-period</FONT></FONT> comparisons of our
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">on-going</FONT> operating results, (ii)&nbsp;the ability to better identify trends in our business and perform related trend analysis, and (iii)&nbsp;an easier way to compare our results of operations against
investor and analyst financial models and valuations, which usually exclude these items. In addition, our definition of Adjusted Earnings per Share may differ from definitions used by other companies and therefore comparability may be limited.
Therefore, when assessing the Company&#146;s operating </P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Quarterly Results of Operations </B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Certain quarterly financial information for the&nbsp;years 2016 and 2015 are set forth below. Such information is derived from our unaudited Consolidated
Financial Statements, prepared on a basis consistent with the Consolidated Financial Statements that include, in our opinion, all normal adjustments necessary for a fair statement of the interim information set forth therein. Operating results for
any quarter are not necessarily indicative of results for any future period. In addition, in view of the significant volatility we have experienced in recent&nbsp;years, the increasingly competitive nature of the markets in which we operate, the
changes in products mix and the currency effects of changes in the composition of sales and production among different geographic regions, we believe that
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">period-to-period</FONT></FONT> comparisons of our operating results should not be relied upon as an indication of future performance. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our quarterly and annual operating results are also affected by a wide variety of other factors that could materially and adversely affect revenues and
profitability or lead to significant variability of operating results, please see &#147;Item 3. Key Information &#151; Risk Factors &#151; Risks Related to Our Operations&#148;. As only a portion of our expenses varies with our revenues, there can
be no assurance that we will be able to reduce costs promptly or adequately in relation to revenue declines to compensate for the effect of any such factors. As a result, unfavorable changes in the above or other factors have in the past and may in
the future adversely affect our operating results. Quarterly results have also been and may be expected to continue to be substantially affected by the cyclical nature of the semiconductor and electronic systems industries, the speed of some process
and manufacturing technology developments, market demand for existing products, the timing and success of new product introductions and the levels of provisions and other unusual charges incurred. Certain additions of our quarterly results will not
total our annual results due to rounding. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B><I>Net revenues </I></B></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>

<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD>

<TD VALIGN="bottom"><B>$</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><B>1,797</B></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><B>$</B></TD>

<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD>

<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><B>%&nbsp;</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><B>11.5</B></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><B>%&nbsp;</B></TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our fourth quarter 2016 net revenues amounted to $1,859&nbsp;million, registering a sequential increase of 3.5%, 30 basis
points above the midpoint of our guidance, due to a sustained demand. The sequential increase resulted from an increase in volume of approximately 6%, partially offset by a decrease of approximately 3% in average selling prices, the latter mainly
due to product mix. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">On a year-over-year basis, our net revenues increased by 11.5% as a result of an approximate 29% increase in volume, which was
partially offset by a 17% decrease in average selling prices, mainly due to the product mix and, to a lesser extent, to price pressure. Excluding businesses undergoing a <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">phase-out</FONT> (mobile legacy products,
camera modules and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">set-top</FONT> box), our revenues increased year-over-year by 12.5%. </P>

<TD VALIGN="top"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:1.00em; text-indent:-1.00em; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Total consolidated net revenues</B></P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><B>$</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><B>1,859</B></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><B>$</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><B>1,797</B></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><B>$</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><B>1,668</B></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><B>3.5</B></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><B>%&nbsp;</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><B>11.5</B></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><B>%&nbsp;</B></TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">On a sequential basis, ADG revenues increased 1.7% driven by automotive microcontrollers and power discrete products. The
increase was due to an increase of about 9% in volumes, partially compensated by a decrease of approximately 7% in average selling prices. AMG revenues increased by 8.2% driven by both MEMS and analog products. The increase in AMG revenues was
principally due to an approximate 8% increase in average selling prices, mainly due to the product mix. MDG revenues increased by 3.8% due to higher volumes of approximately 10%, partially offset by lower average selling prices of about 6%, mainly
impacted by the product mix. Revenues related to specialized image sensors, reported in Others, slightly decreased their revenues sequentially. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">On a
year-over-year basis, fourth quarter net revenues increased 11.5% on strong growth across most product families. ADG revenues increased 12.5% compared to the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">year-ago</FONT> period driven by double-digit growth for
both automotive and power discrete products. The increase was entirely due to higher volumes of about 36%, offset to a large extent by a decline in average selling prices of about 24%, driven by power discrete product mix. AMG revenues increased
17.8% compared to the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">year-ago</FONT> period driven by both strong growth in MEMS and recovery in Analog. The increase was entirely due to higher volumes of about 20%, while average selling prices decreased by about
2%. MDG revenues decreased 0.8% mainly due to lower sales of secure microcontrollers and discontinued businesses <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">(set-top</FONT> box and mobile legacy products). The decrease in average selling prices in MDG of
approximately 16% was not fully compensated by the increased volumes. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">In the fourth quarter of 2016, &#147;Others&#148; included $91&nbsp;million
revenues from our Imaging Product Division, $4&nbsp;million from the sales of Subsystems and $2&nbsp;million from sales of materials and other products not allocated to segments. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B><I>Net Revenues by Market Channel<SUP STYLE="font-size:85%; vertical-align:top">(1)</SUP><SUP STYLE="font-size:85%; vertical-align:top"> </SUP>
</I></B></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>
<TABLE CELLSPACING="0" CELLPADDING="0" WIDTH="84%" BORDER="0" STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE; font-family:Times New Roman; font-size:8pt" ALIGN="center">

to the distributors and representatives that we engage to distribute our products around the world. </TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">By market channel, our
fourth quarter revenues in Distribution amounted to 33% of our total revenues, flat both sequentially and on a year-over-year basis. </P>

<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE; font-family:Times New Roman; font-size:10pt" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR style = "page-break-inside:avoid">
<TD WIDTH="4%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left">(1)</TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top">Net revenues by location of shipment are classified by location of customer invoiced or reclassified by shipment destination in line with customer demand. For example, products ordered by U.S.-based companies to be
invoiced to Asia Pacific affiliates are classified as Asia Pacific revenues. In 2016 we combined the Greater China-South Asia and Japan-Korea revenues, restating prior periods accordingly. Furthermore, the comparison among the different periods may
be affected by shifts in shipment from one location to another, as requested by our customers. </TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">By region of shipment, Asia
Pacific and EMEA grew revenues sequentially 5.5% and 1.4%, respectively, while the Americas declined by 1.1%. The increase in Asia Pacific was mainly due to MEMS and microcontrollers. On a year-over-year basis, Asia Pacific and EMEA grew 18.2% and
5.7%, respectively, while the Americas decreased by 2.8%. The growth in Asia Pacific was mainly due to Imaging, whereas EMEA grew mainly in Automotive. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B><I>Gross profit </I></B></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>

<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The fourth quarter of 2016 gross margin was 37.5%, 50 basis points above the midpoint of our guidance, and
included about 20 basis points of unused capacity charges. On a sequential basis, gross margin increased 170 basis points on improved manufacturing efficiencies, lower unused capacity charges ($4&nbsp;million compared to $11&nbsp;million in the
previous quarter) and improved product mix partially offset principally by normal price pressure. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">On a year-over-year basis, the gross margin increased
by 400 basis points, mainly due to improved manufacturing efficiencies, lower unused capacity charges ($29&nbsp;million in the prior year period) and improved product mix, partially offset by normal price pressure. </P>

from $18&nbsp;million in the prior quarter mainly due to higher R&amp;D funding, and decreasing from $53&nbsp;million in the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">year-ago</FONT> quarter, due to higher R&amp;D funding and the gain realized from the sale

related closure costs, consisting of: (i) $23&nbsp;million relating to employee termination costs on the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">set-top</FONT> box plan and (ii) $1&nbsp;million for other related closure costs. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">In the third quarter of 2016, we recorded $29&nbsp;million of impairment, restructuring charges and other related closure costs, consisting
of: (i) $26&nbsp;million of restructuring charges related to the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">set-top</FONT> box restructuring plan; and (ii) $3&nbsp;million of impairment charges of certain long-lived assets. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">In the fourth quarter of 2015, we recorded $4&nbsp;million of impairment, restructuring charges and other related closure costs, consisting
of: (i) $3&nbsp;million of a <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-monetary</FONT> impairment of intangibles and (ii) $1&nbsp;million charges for the EPS restructuring plan. </P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="line-height:8.0pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:2pt;border-bottom:1px solid #000000;width:10%">&nbsp;</P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE; font-family:Times New Roman; font-size:10pt" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR style = "page-break-inside:avoid">
<TD WIDTH="4%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left">(1)</TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top">Operating result of &#147;Others&#148; includes operating earnings of the Imaging Product Division, Subsystems and other products, as well as items not allocated to the segments such as impairment, restructuring charges
and other related closure costs, unused capacity charges, strategic or special research and development programs and other minor unallocated expenses such as: certain corporate-level operating expenses, patent claims and litigation, and other costs
that are not allocated to the segments. </TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">On a sequential basis, ADG fourth quarter operating income slightly decreased to
$53&nbsp;million from $58&nbsp;million, mainly due to automotive, while power discrete slightly improved. AMG operating income significantly improved to $41&nbsp;million, largely driven by higher sales in both Analog and MEMS. MDG registered an
operating income of $59&nbsp;million, increasing sequentially due to improvements in digital as a result of our cost savings initiatives, while the operating income of microcontrollers remained substantially flat. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">On a year-over-year basis, ADG operating income increased by $9&nbsp;million driven by improved performance in power discrete. AMG operating
income sharply increased from $8&nbsp;million to $41&nbsp;million, driven by both Analog and MEMS. MDG operating income increased by $23&nbsp;million due to improvements in digital as a result of our cost savings initiatives. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B><I>Reconciliation to consolidated operating income (loss) </I></B></P>

<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Impact of Changes in Exchange Rates </B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our results of operations and financial condition can be significantly affected by material changes in the exchange rates between the
U.S.&nbsp;dollar and other currencies, particularly the Euro. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">As a market rule, the reference currency for the semiconductor industry is
the U.S.&nbsp;dollar and the market prices of semiconductor products are mainly denominated in U.S.&nbsp;dollars. However, revenues for some of our </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">49 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">
products (primarily certain of our products sold in Europe) are quoted in currencies other than the U.S. dollar and as such are directly affected by fluctuations in the value of the U.S. dollar.
As a result of currency variations, the appreciation of the Euro compared to the U.S. dollar could increase our level of revenues when reported in U.S. dollars or the depreciation of the Euro compared to the U.S. dollar could decrease our level of
revenues when reported in U.S. dollars. Over time the prices in the industry tend to align to the equivalent amount in U.S. dollars, except that there is a lag between the changes in the currency rate and the adjustment in the price paid in local
currency, which is proportional to the amplitude of the currency swing, and such adjustment could be only partial. Furthermore, certain significant costs incurred by us, such as manufacturing costs, SG&amp;A expenses, and R&amp;D expenses, are
largely incurred in the currency of the jurisdictions in which our operations are located. Given that most of our operations are located in the Euro zone and other non U.S.&nbsp;dollar currency areas, including Singapore, our costs tend to increase
when translated into U.S.&nbsp;dollars when the dollar weakens or to decrease when the U.S.&nbsp;dollar strengthens. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our principal
strategy to reduce the risks associated with exchange rate fluctuations has been to balance as much as possible the proportion of sales to our customers denominated in U.S.&nbsp;dollars with the amount of materials, purchases and services from our
suppliers denominated in U.S.&nbsp;dollars, thereby reducing the potential exchange rate impact of certain variable costs relative to revenues. Moreover, in order to further reduce the exposure to U.S.&nbsp;dollar exchange fluctuations, we have
hedged certain line items on our Consolidated Statements of Income, in particular with respect to a portion of the costs of sales, most of the R&amp;D&nbsp;expenses and certain SG&amp;A expenses, located in the Euro zone, which we account for as
cash flow hedging contracts. We use two&nbsp;different types of hedging contracts: forward and options (including collars). </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our
Consolidated Statements of Income for 2016 included income and expense items translated at the average U.S.&nbsp;dollar exchange rate for the period, plus the impact of the hedging contracts expiring during the period. Our effective average exchange
rate was $1.11 for &#128;1.00 for the full year 2016, compared to $1.17 for &#128;1.00 for the full year 2015. Our effective exchange rate was $1.10 for &#128;1.00 for the fourth quarter of 2016, $1.12 for &#128;1.00 for the third quarter of 2016
and $1.11 for &#128;1.00 for the fourth quarter of 2015. These effective exchange rates reflect the actual exchange rates combined with the impact of cash flow hedging contracts that matured in the period. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The time horizon of our cash flow hedging for manufacturing costs, included within cost of sales, and operating expenses may run up to
24&nbsp;months, for a limited percentage of our exposure to the Euro, depending on currency market circumstances. As of December&nbsp;31, 2016, the outstanding hedged amounts were &#128;795&nbsp;million to cover manufacturing costs and
&#128;524&nbsp;million to cover operating expenses, both at an average exchange rate of about $1.12 to &#128;1.00 (considering the collars at upper strike), maturing over the period from January&nbsp;3, 2017 to July&nbsp;31, 2018. As of
December&nbsp;31, 2016, measured in respect to the exchange rate at period closing of about $1.05 to &#128;1.00, these outstanding hedging contracts and certain expiring contracts covering manufacturing expenses capitalized in inventory resulted in
a deferred loss of approximately $44&nbsp;million before tax, recorded in &#147;Accumulated other comprehensive income (loss)&#148; in the Consolidated Statements of Equity, compared to a deferred loss of approximately $21&nbsp;million before tax at
December&nbsp;31, 2015. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">We also hedge certain manufacturing costs denominated in Singapore dollars (SGD); as of December&nbsp;31, 2016,
the outstanding hedged amounts were SGD 123&nbsp;million at an average exchange rate of about SGD 1.39 to $1.00 maturing over the period from January&nbsp;3, 2017 to November&nbsp;30, 2017. As of December&nbsp;31, 2016, these outstanding hedging
contracts resulted in a deferred loss of approximately $3&nbsp;million before tax, recorded in &#147;Accumulated other comprehensive income (loss)&#148; in the Consolidated Statements of Equity, compared to a deferred loss of approximately
$2&nbsp;million before tax at December&nbsp;31, 2015. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our cash flow hedging policy is not intended to cover our full exposure and is
based on hedging a portion of our exposure in the next four quarters and a declining percentage of our exposure in each quarter thereafter. In 2016, as a result of our cash flow hedging, we recorded a net loss of $9&nbsp;million, consisting of a
loss of about $2&nbsp;million to research and development and a loss of about $7&nbsp;million to cost of sales, while in 2015, we recorded a net loss of $170&nbsp;million. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">In addition to our cash flow hedging, in order to mitigate potential exchange rate risks on our commercial transactions, we purchase and enter
into forward foreign currency exchange contracts and currency options to cover foreign currency exposure in payables or receivables at our affiliates, which we account for as fair value instruments. We may in the future purchase or sell similar
types of instruments. See &#147;Item&nbsp;11. Quantitative and Qualitative Disclosures About Market Risk&#148;. Furthermore, we may not predict in a timely fashion the amount of future transactions in the volatile industry environment. No assurance
may be given that our hedging activities </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">50 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">
will sufficiently protect us against declines in the value of the U.S.&nbsp;dollar. Consequently, our results of operations have been and may continue to be impacted by fluctuations in exchange
rates. The net effect of our consolidated foreign exchange exposure resulted in a net gain of $5&nbsp;million recorded in &#147;Other income and expenses, net&#148; in our 2016 Consolidated Statement of Income compared to a net gain of
$2&nbsp;million recorded in 2015 and a net gain of $4&nbsp;million in 2014. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The assets and liabilities of subsidiaries are, for
consolidation purposes, translated into U.S.&nbsp;dollars at the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">period-end</FONT> exchange rate. Income and expenses, as well as cash flows, are translated at the average exchange rate for the period. The balance
sheet impact, as well as the income statement and cash flow impact, of such translations have been, and may be expected to be, significant from period to period since a large part of our assets and liabilities and activities are accounted for in
Euros as they are located in jurisdictions where the Euro is the functional currency. Adjustments resulting from the translation are recorded directly in equity, and are shown as &#147;Accumulated other comprehensive income (loss)&#148; in the
Consolidated Statements of Equity. At December&nbsp;31, 2016, our outstanding indebtedness was denominated mainly in U.S.&nbsp;dollars and in Euros. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">For a more detailed discussion, see &#147;Item&nbsp;3. Key Information &#151; Risk Factors &#151; Risks Related to Our Operations&#148;. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Impact of Changes in Interest Rates </B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Interest rates may fluctuate upon changes in financial market conditions and material changes can affect our results of operations and
financial condition, since these changes can impact the total interest income received on our cash and cash equivalents and marketable securities, as well as the total interest expense paid on our financial debt. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our interest income (expense), net, as reported in our Consolidated Statements of Income, is the balance between interest income received from
our cash and cash equivalents and marketable securities investments and interest expense paid on our financial liabilities (including the sale without recourse of receivables), <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-cash</FONT> interest expense on the
Senior Convertible Bonds and bank fees (including fees on committed credit lines). Our interest income is dependent upon fluctuations in interest rates, mainly in U.S. dollars and Euros, since we invest primarily on a short-term basis; any increase
or decrease in the market interest rates would mean a proportional increase or decrease in our interest income. Our interest expenses are also dependent upon fluctuations in interest rates, since our financial liabilities include European Investment
Bank Floating Rate Loans at Libor and Euribor plus variable spreads. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">At December&nbsp;31, 2016, our total financial resources, including
cash and cash equivalents and marketable securities, generated an average interest income rate of 1.02%. At the same date, the average interest rate on our outstanding debt was 2.36% including the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-cash</FONT>
effective interest of the convertible bonds, while the average cash interest rate was only 0.85%. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Impact of Changes in Equity Prices </B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">As of December&nbsp;31, 2016, we did not hold any significant equity participations, which could be subject to a material impact in changes in
equity prices. However, we hold equity participations whose carrying value could be reduced due to further losses or impairment charges of our equity-method investments. See Note&nbsp;11 to our Consolidated Financial Statements. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Liquidity and Capital Resources </B></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Treasury
activities are regulated by our policies, which define procedures, objectives and controls. The policies focus on the management of our financial risk in terms of exposure to currency rates and interest rates. Most treasury activities are
centralized, with any local treasury activities subject to oversight from our head treasury office. The majority of our cash and cash equivalents are held in U.S.&nbsp;dollars and Euros and are placed with financial institutions rated at least a
single A long-term rating, meaning at least A3 from Moody&#146;s Investors Service (&#147;Moody&#146;s&#148;) and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">A-</FONT> from Standard&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Poor&#146;s (&#147;S&amp;P&#148;) or Fitch Ratings
(&#147;Fitch&#148;), or better. Marginal amounts are held in other currencies. See &#147;Item&nbsp;11. Quantitative and Qualitative Disclosures About Market Risk&#148;. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our total liquidity and capital resources were $1,964&nbsp;million as of December&nbsp;31, 2016, decreasing compared to $2,106&nbsp;million at
December&nbsp;31, 2015. As of December&nbsp;31, 2016, our total liquidity and capital resources were comprised of $1,629&nbsp;million in cash and cash equivalents and $335&nbsp;million in marketable securities, all considered as current assets. </P>

 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">51 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">As of December&nbsp;31, 2016, marketable securities were $335&nbsp;million invested in U.S.
Government Treasury Bonds with a rating of Aaa/AA+/AAA from Moody&#146;s, S&amp;P and Fitch, respectively, and a weighted average maturity of 3.3 years. The securities are classified as
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">available-for-sale</FONT></FONT> and reported at fair value. This fair value measurement corresponds to a Level&nbsp;1 fair value hierarchy measurement. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Liquidity </B></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">We maintain a significant cash
position and a low <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">debt-to-equity</FONT></FONT> ratio, which provide us with adequate financial flexibility. As in the past, our cash management policy is to finance our investment
needs mainly with net cash generated from operating activities. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">During 2016, our net cash decreased by $142&nbsp;million, due to the net
cash used in investing and financing activities exceeding the net cash from operating activities. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The components of our cash flow for the
last three&nbsp;years are set forth below: </P> <P STYLE="font-size:12pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>
<TABLE CELLSPACING="0" CELLPADDING="0" WIDTH="84%" BORDER="0" STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE; font-family:Times New Roman; font-size:10pt" ALIGN="center">


<TR>
<TD WIDTH="72%"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" WIDTH="6%"></TD>
<TD></TD>

<TD VALIGN="top"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:1.00em; text-indent:-1.00em; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Net cash increase (decrease)</B></P></TD>

<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><I>Net</I> <I>cash</I> <I>from</I> <I>operating</I> <I>activities</I>. Net cash from operating activities is
the sum of (i)&nbsp;net income (loss) adjusted for <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-cash</FONT> items and (ii)&nbsp;changes in net working capital. The net cash from operating activities in 2016 was $1,039&nbsp;million, increasing compared to
$842&nbsp;million in the prior&nbsp;year period, benefitting from both higher net income and more favorable changes in net working capital. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><I>Net</I> <I>cash</I> <I>used</I> <I>in</I> <I>investing</I> <I>activities</I>. Investing activities used $727&nbsp;million of cash in 2016,
increasing from $516&nbsp;million in the prior year, mainly due to payments for the purchase of tangible and intangible assets and $78&nbsp;million payment for business acquisition. Payments for purchase of tangible assets, net of proceeds, totaled
$607&nbsp;million, compared to $467&nbsp;million in 2015. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><I>Net</I> <I>cash</I> <I>from</I> (<I>used</I> <I>in</I>) <I>financing</I>
<I>activities</I>. Net cash used in financing activities was $436&nbsp;million for 2016, compared to the $556&nbsp;million used in 2015. The 2016 amount included $251&nbsp;million in cash dividends paid to stockholders compared to $350&nbsp;million
paid in 2015. </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">52 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><I>Free</I> <I>Cash</I> <I>Flow</I> (<I>non</I> <I>U</I>.<I>S</I>. <I>GAAP</I> <I>measure</I>).
We also present Free Cash Flow, which is a non U.S.&nbsp;GAAP measure, defined as (i)&nbsp;net cash from operating activities plus (ii)&nbsp;net cash used in investing activities, excluding payment for purchases (and proceeds from the sale) of
marketable securities, and net cash variation for joint ventures deconsolidation, which are considered as temporary financial investments. The result of this definition is ultimately net cash from operating activities plus payment for purchase and
proceeds from sale of tangible, intangible and financial assets, proceeds received in the sale of businesses and cash paid for business acquisitions. We believe Free Cash Flow, a non U.S.&nbsp;GAAP measure, provides useful information for investors
and management because it measures our capacity to generate cash from our operating and investing activities to sustain our operations. Free Cash Flow is not a U.S.&nbsp;GAAP measure and does not represent total cash flow since it does not include
the cash flows generated by or used in financing activities. Free Cash Flow reconciles with the total cash flow and the net cash increase (decrease) by including the payment for purchases (and proceeds from the sale) of marketable securities and net
cash variation from joint ventures deconsolidation, the net cash from (used in) financing activities and the effect of changes in exchange rates. In addition, our definition of Free Cash Flow may differ from definitions used by other companies. Free
Cash Flow is determined as follows from our Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows: </P> <P STYLE="font-size:12pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>

$390&nbsp;million excluding the impact of $78&nbsp;million of payment for business acquisitions, net of cash and cash equivalents acquired, compared to positive $327&nbsp;million in 2015. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Capital Resources </B></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><I>Net</I>
<I>Financial</I> <I>Position</I> (<I>non</I> <I>U</I>.<I>S</I>. <I>GAAP</I> <I>measure</I>). Our Net Financial Position represents the difference between our total financial resources and our total financial debt. Our total financial resources
include cash and cash equivalents, marketable securities and short-term deposits, and our total financial debt includes short-term debt, including bank overdrafts, and long-term debt, as represented in our Consolidated Balance Sheets. Net Financial
Position is not a U.S.&nbsp;GAAP measure but we believe it provides useful information for investors and management because it gives evidence of our global position either in terms of net indebtedness or net cash by measuring our capital resources
based on cash and cash equivalents and marketable securities and the total level of our financial indebtedness. In addition, our definition of Net Financial Position may differ from definitions used by other companies and therefore comparability may
be limited. Our Net Financial Position for each period has been determined as follows from our Consolidated Balance Sheets: </P> <P STYLE="font-size:12pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>

<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="line-height:8.0pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:2pt;border-bottom:1px solid #000000;width:10%">&nbsp;</P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE; font-family:Times New Roman; font-size:10pt" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR style = "page-break-inside:avoid">
<TD WIDTH="4%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left">(1)</TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top">The December&nbsp;31, 2014 net financial position was restated following the early adoption of ASU <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">2015-03,</FONT> consisting in a balance sheet reclassification of debt issuance costs
(now reported as deduction of issued debt and not as <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-current</FONT> assets). </TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our Net
Financial Position as of December&nbsp;31, 2016 was a net cash position of $513&nbsp;million, increasing compared to the net cash position of $494&nbsp;million at December&nbsp;31, 2015. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">At December&nbsp;31, 2016, our financial debt was $1,451&nbsp;million, composed of (i)&nbsp;$117&nbsp;million of current portion of long-term
debt and (ii)&nbsp;$1,334&nbsp;million of long-term debt. The breakdown of our total financial debt included: (i)&nbsp;$507&nbsp;million in European Investment Bank loans (the &#147;EIB Loans&#148;), (ii)&nbsp;$926&nbsp;million in the Senior Bonds,
and (iii)&nbsp;$18&nbsp;million in loans from other funding programs and other long-term loans. The EIB Loans are comprised of two&nbsp;long-term amortizing credit facilities as part of our R&amp;D funding programs. The first, signed in 2010, is a
&#128;350&nbsp;million multi-currency loan to support our industrial and R&amp;D programs. It was drawn mainly in U.S.&nbsp;dollars for an amount of $321&nbsp;million and only partially in Euros for an amount of &#128;100&nbsp;million, of which
$213&nbsp;million remained outstanding as of December&nbsp;31, 2016. The second, signed in 2013, is a &#128;350&nbsp;million multi-currency loan which also supports our R&amp;D programs. It was drawn in U.S.&nbsp;dollars for an amount of
$471&nbsp;million, of which $294&nbsp;million is outstanding as of December&nbsp;31, 2016. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Additionally, we had unutilized committed
medium-term credit facilities with core relationship banks of $558&nbsp;million. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our long-term debt contains standard conditions, but
does not impose minimum financial ratios. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">As of December&nbsp;31, 2016, debt payments at redemption value by period were as follows: </P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Senior Bonds were issued on July&nbsp;3, 2014, for a principal amount of $1,000&nbsp;million (Tranche A
for $600&nbsp;million and Tranche B for $400 million), due 2019 and 2021, respectively, for net proceeds of approximately $994&nbsp;million. Tranche A bonds were issued as <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">zero-coupon</FONT> bonds while Tranche B
bonds bear a 1%&nbsp;per annum nominal interest, payable semi-annually. The conversion price at issuance was approximately $12&nbsp;on each tranche. On October&nbsp;3, 2016, the conversion price was adjusted up to 1.24% on each tranche, pursuant
dividend adjustment symmetric provision, which corresponds to 16,491 and 16,366 equivalent shares per each $200,000 bond par value for Tranche A and Tranche B, respectively. The Senior Bonds are convertible by the bondholders if certain conditions
are satisfied on a <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">net-share</FONT> settlement basis, consistent with our intended settlement method. We can also redeem the Senior Bonds prior to their maturity in certain circumstances. Upon initial recognition,
the proceeds were allocated between debt and equity by determining the fair value of the liability component using an income approach. The liability component will accrete to par value until maturity based on the effective interest rate (Tranche A:
2.40% and Tranche B: 3.22%, including 1% p.a. nominal interest). In the computation of diluted earnings per share, the Senior Bonds will be dilutive only for the portion of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">net-share</FONT> settlement underlying the
conversion premium when the conversion option is in the money. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our current ratings with the three major rating agencies that report on us
on a solicited basis, are as follows: S&amp;P: <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">&#147;BBB-&#148;</FONT> with stable outlook; Moody&#146;s: &#147;Ba1&#148; with stable outlook; Fitch: <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">&#147;BBB-&#148;</FONT> with
positive outlook. On February 28, 2017, Fitch affirmed our senior unsecured rating of BBB- and revised the outlook from stable to positive. </P>

<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Operating leases are mainly related to building leases and to equipment. The amount disclosed is composed of minimum payments for future
leases from 2017 to 2021 and thereafter. We lease land, buildings, plants and equipment under operating leases that expire at various dates under <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-cancelable</FONT> lease agreements. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Purchase obligations are primarily comprised of purchase commitments for equipment, for outsourced foundry wafers and for software licenses.
</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Other obligations primarily relate to firm contractual commitments with respect to partnership and cooperation agreements. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Long-term debt obligations mainly consist of bank loans and Senior Bonds. In 2017, we expect to redeem with available cash and cash
equivalents a $112&nbsp;million loan received from European Investment Bank as an annual installment. See &#147;&#151;&nbsp;Net financial position (non U.S.&nbsp;GAAP measure)&#148; above. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Pension obligations amounting to $347&nbsp;million consist of our best estimates of the amounts projected to be payable by us for the pension
and post-employment plans. The final actual amount to be paid and related timing of such payments may vary significantly due to early retirements, terminations and changes in assumptions rates. See Note 15 to our Consolidated Financial Statements.
As part of the Flash divestiture, we retained the obligation to fund the severance payment (<I>trattamento</I> <I>di</I> <I>fine</I> <I>rapporto</I>) due to certain transferred employees by the defined amount of about $7&nbsp;million which qualifies
as a defined benefit plan and was classified as an &#147;other long-term liability&#148; at December&nbsp;31, 2016. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Other long-term
liabilities include future obligations related to our restructuring plans and miscellaneous contractual obligations, as well as <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">earn-out</FONT> for business combinations. In accordance with the authoritative guidance
for accounting for uncertainty in income taxes, as of December&nbsp;31, 2016, we had unrecognized tax benefits of $238&nbsp;million. We do not expect to recognize any of these tax benefits in 2017. We are not, however, able to provide a reasonably
reliable estimate of when these benefits will be recognized. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><I><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Off-Balance</FONT> Sheet Arrangements </I></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">We had no material <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">off-balance</FONT> sheet arrangements at December&nbsp;31, 2016. </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">55 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Financial Outlook: Capital Investment </B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our policy is to modulate our capital spending according to the evolution of the semiconductor market. Based on increased demand and ongoing
strategic initiatives, including new specialized products, we are accelerating our capital spending, aligned to the substantial revenue opportunities we see this year, particularly in the second half. This will result in a temporary increase in
capex spending to $1 billion to $1.1&nbsp;billion for 2017. Specifically, the Company is investing in 300mm <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">front-end</FONT> manufacturing and in <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">back-end</FONT> assembly and test to
support new products, as we anticipate a newly won program to ramp with substantial revenues in the second half of 2017. Our 2017 capex plan is highly frontloaded in timing, aiming to set capacity for the new program starting production in the third
quarter of 2017 and to fulfill the increased demand as early as possible. We see 2017 as a special period, with unique new product opportunities which require internal manufacturing due to technology specialization. We anticipate returning, from
2018 onwards, to our strategic capital spending model with capex at or below 10% of sales through the cycle. The most important of our 2017 capital expenditure projects are expected to be: (a)&nbsp;for our front end facilities: (i)&nbsp;in our 300
mm fab in Crolles, expanding within existing infrastructure capacity to support the production ramp up of a new program from the third quarter of 2017 onward; (ii)&nbsp;mix evolution, and a few selected programs of capacity growth and infrastructure
preparation, mainly in the area of mixed signal and discrete processes, including the Silicon Carbide (SiC) technology; (iii)&nbsp;qualification and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ramp-up</FONT> of technologies in 200 mm in Singapore, Agrate,
Italy and expansion of the 200 mm fab in Catania, Italy; and (iv)&nbsp;quality, safety, maintenance, and productivity and cost savings investments in both 150 mm and 200 mm front end fabs; (b)&nbsp;for our back end facilities: (i)&nbsp;capacity
growth on certain package families, to sustain market demand and secure ramp up of specialty products for strategic customers; (ii)&nbsp;modernization and rationalization of package lines targeting cost savings benefits; and (iii)&nbsp;specific
investments in the areas of factory automation, quality, environment and energy savings; and (c)&nbsp;an overall capacity adjustment in final testing and wafers probing (EWS) to meet increased demand and a changed product mix. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">We will continue to monitor our level of capital spending by taking into consideration factors such as trends in the semiconductor industry
and capacity utilization. We expect to need significant financial resources in the coming&nbsp;years for capital expenditures and for our investments in manufacturing and R&amp;D. We plan to fund our capital requirements from cash provided by
operating activities, available funds and support from third&nbsp;parties, and may have recourse to borrowings under available credit lines and, to the extent necessary or attractive based on market conditions prevailing at the time, the issuance of
debt, convertible bonds or additional equity securities. A substantial deterioration of our economic results, and consequently of our profitability, could generate a deterioration of the cash generated by our operating activities. Therefore, there
can be no assurance that, in future periods, we will generate the same level of cash as in prior&nbsp;years to fund our capital expenditure plans for expanding/upgrading our production facilities, our working capital requirements, our R&amp;D and
manufacturing costs. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">As a result of our exit from the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> joint venture, our exposure is
limited to covering 50% of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson&#146;s</FONT> needs to complete the wind-down, which are estimated to be not material to our consolidated cash flows, based on our current visibility of the <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> liquidation balance. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">We believe that we have the financial resources needed to meet our
currently projected business requirements for the next twelve&nbsp;months, including capital expenditures for our manufacturing activities, working capital requirements, approved dividend payments and the repayment of our debts in line with their
maturity dates. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Impact of Recently Issued U.S. Accounting Standards </B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">See Note 2 to our Consolidated Financial Statements. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Equity-method investments </B></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">See Note 11 to
our Consolidated Financial Statements. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Backlog and Customers </B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">See &#147;Item&nbsp;4. Information on the Company &#151; Backlog&#148;. </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">56 </P>

<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE; font-family:Times New Roman; font-size:10pt" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR style = "page-break-inside:avoid">
<TD WIDTH="9%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><B><A NAME="tx261475_7"></A>Item&nbsp;6.</B></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"><B>Directors, Senior Management and Employees </B></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Directors and Senior Management </B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The management of our Company is entrusted to the Managing Board under the supervision of the Supervisory Board. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B><I>Supervisory Board </I></B></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our Supervisory
Board advises our Managing Board and is responsible for supervising the policies pursued by our Managing Board and the general course of our affairs and business. Our Supervisory Board consists of such number of members as is resolved by our Annual
General Meeting of Shareholders (&#147;AGM&#148;) upon a <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-binding</FONT> proposal of our Supervisory Board, with a minimum of six members. Decisions by our AGM concerning the number and the identity of our
Supervisory Board members are taken by a simple majority of the votes cast at a meeting, provided quorum conditions are met. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our
Supervisory Board was composed of the following nine members as of December&nbsp;31, 2016: </P> <P STYLE="font-size:12pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>

<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Resolutions of our Supervisory Board require the approval of at least three-quarters of its members in office. Our Supervisory Board must meet
upon request by two or more of its members or by our Managing Board. Our Supervisory Board meets at least five times a year, including once per quarter to approve our quarterly, semi-annual and annual accounts and their release. Our Supervisory
Board has adopted a Supervisory Board Charter, which is available on our website (www.st.com). </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Our Supervisory Board may make a proposal
to our annual shareholders&#146; meeting for the suspension or dismissal of one or more of its members. Each member of our Supervisory Board must resign no later than three years after appointment, as described in our Articles of Association, but
may be reappointed following the expiration of his/her term of office. Pursuant to Dutch law, there is no mandatory retirement age for members of our Supervisory Board. Members of the Supervisory Board may be suspended or dismissed by our annual
shareholders&#146; meeting. Certain of our Supervisory Board members are proposed by and may retain certain relationships with our direct or indirect shareholders represented through our major shareholder. See &#147;Item 7. Major Shareholders and
Related Party Transactions &#151; Major Shareholders.&#148; </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><I>Biographies of our Current Supervisory Board Members </I></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Maurizio Tamagnini has been a member and the Chairman of our Supervisory Board since June 2014. He serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s
Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee and chairs its Compensation Committee and Strategic Committee. Mr. Tamagnini is currently Chief Executive Officer of FSI Sgr Spa, an asset management company participated, with a significant stake, among
others, by Cassa depositi e&nbsp;prestiti Spa (CDP), which is 80% controlled by the Italian Government. FSI Sgr Spa manages a fund, not yet established, specialized on growth equity investments in Italian midmarket companies with development
potential. He is non-executive Chairman of FSI Investimenti Spa, which is controlled 77% by CDP. Until 31&nbsp;March 2016, Mr. Tamagnini was Chief Executive Officer and Chairman of the Investment Committee of Fondo Strategico Italiano Spa (now CDP
Equity Spa), an investment company sponsored by CDP. He was previously Southern European Manager of the Corporate &amp; Investments Banking division of Bank of America Merrill Lynch and a member of the Executive Committee of Bank of America Merrill
Lynch for the EMEA region. Mr. Tamagnini has gained over 25 years of experience in the financial sector specializing in the areas of Corporate Finance, Private Equity, Debt and Equity. Until 21 April 2016, he was Chairman of the Joint Venture
between CDP Equity and Qatar Holding (IQ Made in Italy Investment Company Spa) with capital endowment of </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">57 </P>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">
up to &#128;2 billion in total for investments in the food, brands, furniture &amp; design and tourism sectors. Mr.&nbsp;Tamagnini is also a member of the Advisory Board of RDIF (the Russian
Direct Investment Fund). He holds a degree in International Monetary Economics from Bocconi University in Milan and has also studied at the Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute &#151; Troy in New York, USA. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Didier Lombard has been a member of our Supervisory Board since 2004 and has been its Vice-Chairman since June&nbsp;2014. He was the
Supervisory Board&#146;s Chairman from 2011 until 2014. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Compensation Committee, Strategic Committee and Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard was appointed Chairman
and Chief Executive Officer of Orange (formerly France Telecom) in March&nbsp;2005, and served as Chief Executive Officer until February&nbsp;2010 and Chairman until March&nbsp;2011. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard began his career in the Research and Development
division of Orange in 1967. From 1989 to 1990, he served as scientific and technological director at the Ministry of Research and Technology. From 1991 to 1998, he served as General Director for industrial strategies at the French Ministry of
Economy, Finances and Industry, and from 1999 to 2003 he served as an Ambassador at large for foreign investments in France and as President of the French Agency for International Investments. From 2003 through February&nbsp;2005, he served as
Orange&#146;s Senior Executive Vice President in charge of technologies, strategic partnerships and new usages and as a member of Orange&#146;s Executive Committee. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard was also a member of the board of directors of Thales until May
2014. He is also the Chairman of the board of directors of Technicolor (previously Thomson), one of our customers, as well as a member of the supervisory board of Radiall. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard was also a member until his resignation on November&nbsp;15,
2006 of the supervisory board of ST Holding, our largest shareholder. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard is a graduate of the Ecole Polytechnique and the Ecole Nationale Sup&eacute;rieure des T&eacute;l&eacute;communications. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Janet G. Davidson has been a member of our Supervisory Board since June&nbsp;2013. She serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Audit Committee
and Strategic Committee. She began her career in 1979 as a member of the Technical Staff of Bell Laboratories, Lucent Technologies (as of 2006 Alcatel Lucent), and served from 1979 through 2011 in several key positions, most recently as Chief
Strategy Officer (2005 &#150; 2006), Chief Compliance Officer (2006 &#150; 2008) and EVP Quality&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Customer Care (2008 &#150; 2011). From 2005 through 2012, Ms.&nbsp;Davidson was a member of the Lehigh University Board of Trustees. In
2007 she served on the Riverside Symphonia Board of Trustees and in 2005 and 2006, Ms.&nbsp;Davidson was a member of the Liberty Science Center Board of Trustees. Ms.&nbsp;Davidson was a member of the board of the Alcatel Lucent Foundation from 2011
until 2014. Ms.&nbsp;Davidson is also a member of the board of directors of Millicom since April 2016. Ms.&nbsp;Davidson is a graduate of the Georgia Institute of Technology (Georgia Tech), Atlanta, GA, USA, and Lehigh University, Bethlehem, PA, USA
and holds a Master&#146;s degree in Electrical Engineering. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Nicolas Dufourcq has been a member of our Supervisory Board since May 2015.
He serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Compensation Committee, Strategic Committee and Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee. Mr.&nbsp;Dufourcq is a graduate of HEC (Hautes Etudes Commerciales) and ENA (Ecole Nationale
d&#146;Administration). He began his career at the French Ministry of Finance and Economics before joining the Ministry of Health and Social affairs in 1992. In 1994, he joined France Telecom, where he created the Multimedia division, before going
on to chair Wanadoo, the firm&#146;s listed Internet and Yellow Pages subsidiary. After joining the Capgemini Group in 2003, he was made responsible for the Central and Southern Europe region, successfully leading their financial turnaround. He was
appointed Chief Financial Officer of the Group and member of the Executive Committee in September 2004. In 2005, he was named deputy Chief Executive Officer in charge of finance, risk management, IT, delivery, purchases and LEAN program and, in
2007, also in charge of the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">follow-up</FONT> of the group&#146;s major contracts. On February&nbsp;7, 2013, Mr.&nbsp;Dufourcq was appointed Chief Executive Officer of Bpifrance (Banque Publique
d&#146;Investissement), which is indirectly controlled by the French Government and is one of the indirect shareholders of ST Holding. Mr.&nbsp;Dufourcq is also a member of the Supervisory Board of Euler Hermes Group. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Heleen Kersten has been a member of our Supervisory Board since June 2014. She serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Audit Committee and
Compensation Committee and chairs its Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee. Ms.&nbsp;Kersten is a partner at Stibbe in Amsterdam, where she held the position of managing partner from 2008 to 2013. Stibbe is a Benelux law firm with offices
in Amsterdam, Brussels, Luxembourg, London, New York, Dubai and Hong Kong. She began her career in 1989 with Stibbe before joining Davis Polk in New York and London (1992-1993). After her return to Stibbe Amsterdam, she rose through the ranks to
become a partner in 1997. As a member of the Bar of Amsterdam since 1989, Ms.&nbsp;Kersten specializes in mergers and acquisitions, equity capital markets, corporate law and corporate governance. Ms.&nbsp;Kersten was a supervisory board member of
the Dutch listed bank Van Lanschot N.V. until May 2015 and the Chairman of the supervisory board of Egeria Investment B.V. until April 2016. She is currently a supervisory </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">58 </P>

<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">
board member of the Rijksmuseum (Stichting Het Rijksmuseum), since 2015. She is also a board member of the Foundation Donors of the Royal Concertgebouw Orchestra (Stichting Donateurs Koninklijk
Concertgebouworkest), since 2010. Ms.&nbsp;Kersten holds master&#146;s degrees in Dutch law and tax law, both from Leiden University in the Netherlands. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Jean-Georges Malcor has been a member of our Supervisory Board since May&nbsp;2011. He serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Audit Committee.
Mr.&nbsp;Malcor is the Chief Executive Officer of CGG. He is a graduate of Ecole Centrale de Paris. He also holds a Master of Sciences degree from Stanford University, and a Doctorat from Ecole des Mines. Mr.&nbsp;Malcor began his career at the
Thales group as an acoustic engineer in the Underwater Activities division where he was particularly in charge of hydrophone and geophone design and towed streamer programs. He then moved to the Sydney based Thomson Sintra Pacific Australia,
becoming Managing Director of the company in 1990. Back in France, he became Director of Marketing and Communications (1991), then Director, Foreign Operations of Thomson Sintra Activit&eacute;s Sous Marines (1993). In 1996, he was appointed
Managing Director of Thomson Marconi Sonar Australia which was, in addition to its military activities, the lead developing company for the solid geophysical streamer. In 1999, Mr.&nbsp;Malcor became the first Managing Director of the newly formed
joint venture Australian Defense Industry. During this time he operated the Sydney based Woolloomooloo Shipyard (the largest dry dock in the southern hemisphere). In 2002, he became Senior Vice President, International Operations of Thales
International. From 2004 to 2009, he was Senior Vice President in charge of the Naval Division, supervising all naval activities in Thales including ship design, building and maintenance. In January&nbsp;2009, he became Senior Vice President, in
charge of the Aerospace Division. In June&nbsp;2009, he moved to the position of Senior Vice President, Continental Europe, Turkey, Russia, Asia, Africa, Middle East, and Latin America. Mr.&nbsp;Malcor joined CGG in January&nbsp;2010 as President
and became CEO on June&nbsp;30, 2010. Since June&nbsp;2013, Mr.&nbsp;Malcor has been a member of the Supervisory Board (as well as its Appointment and Compensation Committee) of the Fives Group. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Salvatore Manzi has been a member of our Supervisory Board since May 2016. He serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Compensation Committee
and Strategic Committee. Mr.&nbsp;Manzi is the founder and managing director (since 2007) of Schema31 S.p.A., a company providing innovation service as a business process outsourcer for public administrations and large private customers. During the
course of his professional career, Mr.&nbsp;Manzi directed several Italian software companies, where he was responsible for ICT projects in the areas of enterprise management, finance and control, training and R&amp;D. A primary player in one of the
major SAP projects in Italy (Rete Ferroviaria Italiana &#150; RFI SpA), Mr.&nbsp;Manzi carries a multi-year international executive management experience in the direction of R&amp;D operations and enterprise ICT projects. Mr.&nbsp;Manzi was a member
of the supervisory board of ST Holding NV from 2014 until May&nbsp;25, 2016. Mr.&nbsp;Manzi holds a master degree in electrical engineering from the Florence University, Italy, and is a member of the National Board of Engineers, section of Rome (IT,
construction, environmental and industrial engineering). </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Alessandro Rivera has been a member of our Supervisory Board since May 2011.
Mr.&nbsp;Rivera serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Audit Committee and Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee. He has been the Head of Directorate IV &#147;Financial Sector Policy and Regulation Legal Affairs&#148; at the Department of
the Treasury, Ministry of Economy and Finance, since 2008. He served as Head of Unit in the Department of the Treasury from 2000 to 2008 and was responsible for a variety of policy matters: financial services and markets, banking foundations,
accounting, finance, corporate governance and auditing. Since 2008, Mr.&nbsp;Rivera has been the Government representative in the &#147;Consiglio Superiore&#148; of the Bank of Italy, and in the Financial Services Committee. Since 2013 he has been a
member of the Board of Directors and Compensation Committee of Cassa Depositi e Prestiti. From 2011 to 2014 he was a member of the Board of Directors and Compensation Committee of Poste Italiane S.p.A.. From 2008 to 2011 he was a member of the
European Securities Committee. He was a member of the Accounting Regulatory Committee from 2002 to 2008 and a member of the Audit Regulatory Committee from 2005 to 2008. He served on the board of Italia Lavoro S.p.A. from 2005 to 2008 and was a
member of the Audit Committee and the Compensation Committee. Mr.&nbsp;Rivera was also the Chairman of the Audit Committee of the &#147;Fondo nazionale di garanzia degli intermediari finanziari&#148; (Italian investor compensation scheme) from 2003
to 2008. From 2001 to 2010, he was the Project Leader and Deputy Project Leader in several twinning projects with Eastern European Countries (the Russian Federation, the Czech Republic, Lithuania, and Bulgaria). He also served on the board of
Mediocredito del Friuli &#151; Venezia Giulia S.p.A from 2001 to 2003. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Martine Verluyten has been a member of our Supervisory Board since
May&nbsp;2012. Ms.&nbsp;Verluyten serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Audit Committee and has been its Chair since April&nbsp;22, 2013. Until 2011,&nbsp;Ms.&nbsp;Verluyten acted as CFO of Umicore N.V. based in Brussels. Previously she was CFO of
Mobistar N.V. (2001-2006), having initially joined Mobistar in 2000 as Group Controller. She had earlier worked at Raychem since 1976, holding various management positions during her 23&nbsp;year tenure, from Manager European
</P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">59 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">
Consolidations (1976-1979), to General Accounting Manager based in the US (1979-1983). She was then promoted to Division Controller Telecom Division Europe from 1983 to 1990. In 1990, she was
appointed Finance&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Administration Director back in Europe, then in 1995, Europe Controller Finance&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Administration Director until 1999. Ms.&nbsp;Verluyten is also member of the board of directors of Thomas Cook plc, 3i
plc and GBL (group Bruxelles Lambert). Ms.&nbsp;Verluyten began her career in 1973 at KPMG as an Auditor. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B><I>Supervisory Board Committees </I></B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; text-indent:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><I>Membership</I> <I>and</I> <I>Attendance</I>. As of December&nbsp;31, 2016, the composition of the four standing committees of our
Supervisory Board was as follows: (i)&nbsp;Ms.&nbsp;Martine Verluyten is the Chair of the Audit Committee, and Ms.&nbsp;Janet&nbsp;G.&nbsp;Davidson, Ms.&nbsp;Heleen Kersten, Mr.&nbsp;Jean-Georges Malcor and Mr.&nbsp;Alessandro Rivera are members of
the Audit Committee; (ii)&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Maurizio Tamagnini is the Chairman of the Compensation Committee, and Mr.&nbsp;Nicolas Dufourcq, Ms.&nbsp;Heleen Kersten, Mr.&nbsp;Didier Lombard and Mr.&nbsp;Salvatore Manzi are members of the Compensation
Committee; (iii)&nbsp;Ms.&nbsp;Heleen Kersten is the Chair of the Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee, and Messrs.&nbsp;Nicolas Dufourcq, Didier Lombard, Alessandro Rivera and Maurizio Tamagnini are members of the Nominating and Corporate
Governance Committee; and (iv)&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Maurizio Tamagnini is the Chairman of the Strategic Committee, and Ms.&nbsp;Janet G. Davidson and Messrs. Nicolas Dufourcq, Didier Lombard and Salvatore Manzi are members of the Strategic Committee. </P>

<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE; font-family:Times New Roman; font-size:8pt" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
