<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD></TR>
<TR STYLE="page-break-inside:avoid ; font-family:Times New Roman; font-size:10pt">
<TD VALIGN="top"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:1.00em; text-indent:-1.00em; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Total</B></P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><B>45</B></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><B>44</B></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD></TR>
<TR STYLE="font-size:1px; ">
<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD>&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD>&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><I><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> SA, in liquidation </I></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">On February&nbsp;3, 2009, the Company announced the closing of a transaction to combine the businesses of Ericsson Mobile Platforms and <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-NXP</FONT> Wireless into a new venture, named <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson.</FONT> As part of the transaction, the Company received an interest in <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT>
Holding AG in which the Company owned 50% plus a controlling share. In 2010, <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> Holding AG was merged in <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> SA. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company evaluated that <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> SA was a variable interest entity (VIE). The Company determined that it
controlled <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> SA and therefore consolidated <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> SA. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">On
September&nbsp;9, 2013, the Company sold 1 <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> SA share to Ericsson for its nominal value changing the ownership structure of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> SA to bring both
partners to an equal ownership proportion. As a result and in combination with the new shareholder agreement, the Company lost the control of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> SA and as such
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> SA was deconsolidated from the Company&#146;s consolidated financial statements. The deconsolidation of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> SA did not result in a gain or loss for
the Company. The fair value of the Company&#146;s retained noncontrolling interest was evaluated at $55&nbsp;million. In addition, the Company and its partner signed funding commitment letters, capped at $149&nbsp;million for each partner, to the
residual joint wind-down operations to ensure solvency. These were not drawn as of December&nbsp;31, 2016. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Before the deconsolidation of <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> SA, certain assets and companies of the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> SA group of companies were transferred to both partners for their net book value which was representative of
their fair value. The transactions did not result in cash exchange between the partners. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> SA entered
into liquidation on April&nbsp;15, 2014. For the years 2016, 2015 and 2014, the line &#147;Income (loss) on equity-method investments&#148; in the Company&#146;s consolidated statement of income included a profit of $1&nbsp;million, $1&nbsp;million
and $9&nbsp;million, respectively, in relation with <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> SA. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B><I>Cost-method investments </I></B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Cost-method investments as at December&nbsp;31, 2016 and 2015 are equity securities with no readily determinable fair value. It includes principally the
Company&#146;s investment in DNP Photomask Europe S.p.A (&#147;DNP&#148;). The Company has identified the joint venture as a VIE, but has determined that it is not the primary beneficiary. The significant activities of DNP revolve around the
creation of masks and development of high level mask technology. The Company does not have the power to direct such activities. The Company&#146;s current maximum exposure to loss as a result of its involvement with the joint venture is limited to
its investment. The Company has not provided additional financial support in years 2016 and 2015 and currently has no requirement or intent to provide further financial support to the joint venture. </P>
<P STYLE="font-size:18pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Long-term State receivables include receivables related to funding and receivables related to tax refund. Funding
are mainly public grants to be received from governmental agencies in Italy and France as part of long-term research and development, industrialization and capital investment projects. Long-term receivables related to tax refund correspond to tax
benefits claimed by the Company in certain of its local tax jurisdictions, for which collection is expected beyond one year. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">In 2016, the Company entered
into a factoring transaction to accelerate the realization in cash of some <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-current</FONT> assets. As at December&nbsp;31, 2016, $127&nbsp;million of the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-current</FONT> assets
were sold without recourse, compared to $96&nbsp;million as at December&nbsp;31, 2015, with a financial cost of less than $1&nbsp;million for both periods. </P> <P STYLE="font-size:18pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>

<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD></TR>

<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD>&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD>&nbsp;</TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The European Investment Bank&#146;s loans denominated in Euros, but drawn in U.S. dollars, are classified as U.S.
dollar-denominated debt. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">On July&nbsp;3, 2014, the Company issued $1,000&nbsp;million in principal amount of dual tranche senior unsecured convertible
bonds (Tranche A for $600&nbsp;million and Tranche B for $400 million), due 2019 and 2021, respectively. Tranche A bonds were issued as <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">zero-coupon</FONT> bonds while Tranche B bonds bear a 1% per annum nominal
interest, payable semi-annually. The conversion price at issuance was approximately $12 dollar, equivalent to a 30% and a 31% premium, respectively, on each tranche. On October&nbsp;3, 2016, the conversion price was adjusted up to 1.24% on each
tranche, pursuant to a dividend adjustment symmetric provision, which corresponds to 16,491 and 16,366 equivalent shares per each $200,000 bond par value for Tranche A and Tranche B, respectively. The bonds are convertible by the bondholders if
certain conditions are satisfied on a <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">net-share</FONT> settlement basis, consistent with the Company&#146;s intended settlement method. The Company can also redeem the bonds prior to their maturity in certain
circumstances. The net proceeds from the bond offering were approximately $994&nbsp;million, after deducting issuance costs payable by the Company. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Proceeds were allocated between debt and equity by measuring first the liability component and then determining the equity component as a residual amount. The
liability component was measured at fair value based on a discount rate adjustment technique (income approach), which corresponds to a Level&nbsp;3 fair value hierarchy measurement. The fair value of the liability component at initial recognition
totalled $878&nbsp;million and was estimated by calculating the present value of cash flows using a discount rate of 2.40% and 3.22% (including 1% per annum nominal interest), respectively, on each tranche, which were determined to be consistent
with the market rates at the time for similar instruments with no conversion rights. An amount of $121&nbsp;million, net of allocated issuance costs of $1&nbsp;million, was recorded in shareholders&#146; equity as the value of the conversion
features of the instruments. Unamortized debt discount and issuance costs totalled $74&nbsp;million as at December&nbsp;31, 2016 and $95&nbsp;million as at December&nbsp;31, 2015. </P>

<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B><I>Credit facilities </I></B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company had unutilized committed medium-term credit facilities with core relationship banks totalling $558&nbsp;million as of December&nbsp;31, 2016. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company also has two long-term amortizing credit facilities with the European Investment Bank as part of R&amp;D funding programs. The first one, signed
on September&nbsp;27, 2010 as a &#128;350&nbsp;million multi-currency loan for R&amp;D programs in Europe, was drawn mainly in U.S. dollars for an amount of $321&nbsp;million and only partially in Euros for an amount of &#128;100&nbsp;million, of
which $213&nbsp;million remained outstanding as of December&nbsp;31, 2016. The second one, signed on March&nbsp;12, 2013, a &#128;350&nbsp;million multi-currency loan which also supports R&amp;D programs, was drawn in U.S. dollars for
$471&nbsp;million, of which $294&nbsp;million was outstanding as of December&nbsp;31, 2016. </P> <P STYLE="font-size:18pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE; font-family:Times New Roman; font-size:10pt" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR style = "page-break-inside:avoid">
<TD WIDTH="4%">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD WIDTH="4%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><B>15.</B></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"><B>POST-EMPLOYMENT AND OTHER LONG-TERM EMPLOYEES BENEFITS </B></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company and its subsidiaries have a number
of defined benefit pension plans, mainly unfunded, and other long-term employees&#146; benefits covering employees in various countries. The defined benefit plans provide pension benefits based on years of service and employee compensation levels.
The other long-term employees&#146; plans provide benefits due during the employees&#146; period of service after certain seniority levels. The Company uses a December&nbsp;31 measurement date for its plans. Eligibility is generally determined in
accordance with local statutory requirements. For Italian termination indemnity plan (&#147;TFR&#148;), generated before July&nbsp;1, 2007, the Company continues to measure the vested benefits to which Italian employees are entitled as if they left
the company immediately as of December&nbsp;31, 2016, in compliance with U.S. GAAP guidance on determining vested benefit obligations for defined benefit pension plans. </P>

categories of investments held by the plan against a number of various potential economic scenarios. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company&#146;s pension plan asset allocation at

<TD VALIGN="top"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:1.00em; text-indent:-1.00em; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>December&nbsp;31, 2015</B></P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><B>102</B></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><B>&nbsp;</B></TD></TR>
<TR STYLE="font-size:1px; ">
<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD>&nbsp;</TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company&#146;s investment strategy for its pension plans is to optimize the long-term investment return on plan assets in
relation to the liability structure to maintain an acceptable level of risk while minimizing the cost of providing pension benefits and maintaining adequate funding levels in accordance with applicable rules in each jurisdiction. The Company&#146;s
practice is to periodically conduct a review in each subsidiary of its asset allocation strategy, in such a way that the asset allocation is in line with the targeted asset allocation with reasonable boundaries. The Company&#146;s asset portfolios
are managed in such a way as to achieve adapted diversity and in certain jurisdictions they are entirely managed by the multi-employer funds. The Company does not manage any assets internally. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">After considering the funded status of the Company&#146;s defined benefit plans, movements in the discount rate, investment performance and related tax
consequences, the Company may choose to make contributions to its pension plans in any given year in excess of required amounts. The Company&#146;s contributions to plan assets were $27&nbsp;million in 2016, $28&nbsp;million in 2015 and the Company
expects to contribute cash of $28&nbsp;million in 2017. </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">F-33 </P>

<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right">36</TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right">3</TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD></TR>
<TR STYLE="page-break-inside:avoid ; font-family:Times New Roman; font-size:10pt">
<TD VALIGN="top"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:1.00em; text-indent:-1.00em; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">From 2022 to 2026</P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right">233</TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right">27</TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company has certain defined contribution plans, which accrue benefits for employees on a
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">pro-rata</FONT> basis during their employment period based on their individual salaries. The Company accrued benefits related to defined contribution pension plans of $16&nbsp;million as of December&nbsp;31, 2016 and
December&nbsp;31, 2015. The annual cost of these plans amounted to approximately $70&nbsp;million in both 2016 and 2015 and $81&nbsp;million in 2014. </P> <P STYLE="font-size:18pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE; font-family:Times New Roman; font-size:10pt" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR style = "page-break-inside:avoid">
<TD WIDTH="4%">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD WIDTH="4%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><B>16.</B></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"><B>SHAREHOLDERS&#146; EQUITY </B></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:6%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>16.1 Outstanding shares </B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The authorized share capital of the Company is Euro 1,810&nbsp;million consisting of 1,200,000,000 common shares and 540,000,000 preference shares, each with a
nominal value of &#128;1.04. As at December&nbsp;31, 2016 the number of shares of common stock issued was 911,030,420 shares (910,967,920 at December&nbsp;31, 2015). </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">As of December&nbsp;31, 2016 the number of shares of common stock outstanding was 883,410,506 (878,537,339 at December&nbsp;31, 2015). </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:6%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>16.2 Preference shares </B></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The 540,000,000
preference shares, when issued, will entitle a holder to full voting rights and to a preferential right to dividends and distributions upon liquidation. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">On January&nbsp;22, 2007, an option agreement was concluded between the Company and Stichting Continu&iuml;teit ST. This option agreement provides for the
issuance of 540,000,000 preference shares. The Foundation can acquire preference shares upon payment of at least 25% of the par value of the preference shares to be issued in the event of actions which the board of the Foundation determines would be
contrary to our interests, our shareholders and our other stakeholders and which in the event of a creeping acquisition or offer for our common shares are not supported by our Managing Board and Supervisory Board. The preference shares may remain
outstanding for no longer than two years. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">In October 2016, the Company extended for another <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">10-year</FONT> period the
existing option agreement with Stichting Continuite&iuml;t ST. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">There were no preference shares issued as of December&nbsp;31, 2016 and December&nbsp;31,
2015 respectively. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:6%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>16.3 Treasury stock </B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Following the authorization by the Supervisory Board, announced on April&nbsp;2, 2008, to repurchase up to 30&nbsp;million shares of its common stock, the
Company acquired 29,520,220 shares in 2008, also reflected at cost, as a reduction of the parent company stockholders&#146; equity. Additionally, pursuant to a resolution passed at the shareholders&#146; meeting held on June&nbsp;13, 2014, the
Company repurchased 20,000,000 shares in 2014 under the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">buy-back</FONT> program. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">As of December&nbsp;31, 2016, the
Company owned a number of treasury shares equivalent to 27,619,914 compared to 32,430,581 as of December&nbsp;31, 2015. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The treasury shares have been
designated for allocation under the Company&#146;s share based remuneration programs of unvested shares. As of December&nbsp;31, 2016, 35,300,306 of these treasury shares were transferred to employees under the Company&#146;s share based
remuneration programs, of which 4,810,667 in the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2016. </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">F-34 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:6%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>16.4 Unvested share awards for the Supervisory Board </B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">On an annual basis and until the year 2012, the Compensation Committee (on behalf of the Supervisory Board and with its approval) used to grant stock-based
awards (the options to acquire common shares in the share capital of the Company) to the members and professionals of the Supervisory Board (&#147;The Supervisory Board Plan&#148;). The awards were granted at the nominal value of the share of
&#128;1.04 (exercise price of the option). The options granted under the Supervisory Board Plan vest and become exercisable immediately, while the shares resulting from these awards vest and therefore become available for trade evenly over three
years (one third every year), with no market, performance or service conditions. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The table below summarizes grants under the outstanding stock award
plans as authorized by the Compensation Committee: </P> <P STYLE="font-size:12pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The total intrinsic value of options exercised during the year 2016 amounted to less than $1&nbsp;million compared to
$1&nbsp;million for the year 2015. The total intrinsic value of options outstanding as of December&nbsp;31, 2016 amounted to $3&nbsp;million. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">At the
Company&#146;s Annual General Meeting of Shareholders held on 21&nbsp;June 2013, it was resolved to abolish and terminate the stock-based compensation for the Supervisory Board members and professionals. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:6%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>16.5 Unvested share awards for the employees </B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">On an annual basis, the Compensation Committee (on behalf of the Supervisory Board and with its approval) grants stock-based awards to the senior executives
along with selected employees (the &#147;Employee Plan&#148;). The awards are granted for services under the Employee Plan. There are two types of unvested shares: (1)&nbsp;shares granted to employees, which are subject only to service conditions
and vest over the requisite service period, and (2)&nbsp;shares granted to senior executives, whose vesting is subject to performance conditions. For the plans 2013, 2014 and 2015, the performance conditions consisted of two external targets (sales
evolution and operating income compared to a basket of competitors) weighting for two third of the total number of awards granted and </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">F-35 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">
of one internal target (cash flow compared to budget for the plans 2013 and 2014 and return on net assets compared to budget for the plan 2015), weighting for one third of the total number of
awards granted. For the plan 2016, the performance conditions consisted of two external targets (sales evolution and operating income compared to a basket of competitors) weighting for 80% of the total number of awards granted and of two internal
targets (days of sales outstanding compared to the budget and return on net assets compared to budget), weighting for 20% of the total number of awards granted. All the awards vest over a three year service period (32% as of the first anniversary of
the grant, 32% as of the second anniversary of the grant and 36% as of the third anniversary of the grant). In addition, in 2013, 2014 and 2015 there was a Special Bonus granted to the Company&#146;s CEO. </P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The grant date fair value of unvested shares granted to the CEO under the 2013 CEO Special Bonus Plan was $9.35. On the 2013
CEO Special Bonus Plan, the fair value of the unvested shares granted reflected the market price of the shares at the date of the grant. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The grant date
weighted average fair value of unvested shares granted to employees under the 2013 Employee Plan was $9.55. On April&nbsp;28, 2014, the Compensation Committee approved the statement that one performance condition was fully met. Consequently, the
compensation expense recorded on the 2013 Employee Plan reflects the statement that one third of the awards granted will fully vest, as far as the service condition is met. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The grant date fair value of unvested shares granted to the CEO under the 2014 CEO Special Bonus Plan was $9.35. On the 2014 CEO Special Bonus Plan, the fair
value of the unvested shares granted reflected the market price of the shares at the date of the grant. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The grant date weighted average fair value of
unvested shares granted to employees under the 2014 Employee Plan was $9.23. On March&nbsp;24, 2015, the Compensation Committee approved the statement that with respect to the shares subject to performance conditions, one performance condition was
fully met. Consequently, the compensation expense recorded on the 2014 Employee Plan reflects the statement that &#150; for the portion of shares subject to performance conditions &#150; one third of the awards granted will fully vest, as far as the
service condition is met. </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">F-36 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The grant date fair value of unvested shares granted to employees under the 2015 Employee Plan was $7.62. On
April&nbsp;26, 2016, the Compensation Committee approved the statement that with respect to the shares subject to performance conditions, one performance condition was fully met. Consequently, the compensation expense recorded on the 2015 Employee
Plan reflects the statement that &#150; for the portion of shares subject to performance conditions &#150; one third of the awards granted will fully vest, as far as the service condition is met. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The grant date weighted average fair value of unvested shares granted to employees under the 2016 Employee Plan was $6.37. Moreover, for the portion of the
shares subject to performance conditions (3,074,320 shares) the Company estimates the number of awards expected to vest by assessing the probability of achieving the performance conditions. At December&nbsp;31, 2016, a final determination of the
achievement of the performance conditions had not yet been made by the Compensation Committee of the Supervisory Board. However, the Company has estimated that 45% of the awards subject to performance conditions are expected to vest. Consequently,
the compensation expense recorded for the 2016 Employee Plan reflects the vesting of 45% of the awards granted with performance conditions, subject to the service condition being met. The assumption of the expected number of awards to be vested upon
achievement of the performance conditions is subject to changes based on the final measurement of the conditions, which is expected to occur in the first half of 2017. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The following table illustrates the classification of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">pre-payroll</FONT> tax and social contribution stock-based compensation

<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Compensation cost, excluding payroll tax and social contribution, capitalized as part of inventory was $2&nbsp;million at each of December&nbsp;31, 2016, 2015
and 2014. As of December&nbsp;31, 2016 there was $35&nbsp;million of total unrecognized compensation cost related to the grant of unvested shares, which is expected to be recognized over a weighted average period of approximately 9 months. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The total deferred income tax benefit recognized in the consolidated statements of income related to unvested share-based compensation expense amounted to
$2&nbsp;million, $2&nbsp;million and $1&nbsp;million for the years ended December&nbsp;31, 2016, 2015 and 2014, respectively. </P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:6%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>16.7 Dividends </B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Annual General Meeting of Shareholders held on May&nbsp;25, 2016, authorized the distribution of a cash dividend of $0.24 per outstanding share of the
Company&#146;s common stock, to be distributed in quarterly installments of $0.06 in each of the second, third and fourth quarters of 2016 and first quarter of 2017. $53&nbsp;million corresponding to the first installment, $53&nbsp;million
corresponding to the second installment and $47&nbsp;million corresponding to the third installment were paid during 2016. The amount of $59&nbsp;million presented in the line &#147;Dividends payable to stockholders&#148; in the consolidated balance
sheet as of December&nbsp;31, 2016 is composed of the fourth installment of $53&nbsp;million and the remaining portion of the third installment of $6&nbsp;million, both to be paid in the first half of 2017. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Annual General Meeting of Shareholders held on May&nbsp;27, 2015 authorized the distribution of a cash dividend of US$0.40 per outstanding share of the
Company&#146;s common stock, to be distributed in quarterly </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">F-39 </P>

installments of US$0.10 in each of the second, third and fourth quarters of 2015 and first quarter of 2016. $88&nbsp;million corresponding to the first installment, $88&nbsp;million corresponding
to the second installment and $78&nbsp;million corresponding to the third installment were paid during 2015. The remaining portion of $9&nbsp;million related to the third installment and the fourth installment of $88&nbsp;million were paid in the
first half of 2016. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Supervisory Board held on December&nbsp;4, 2014 authorized the distribution of a semi-annual cash dividend per common share of
$0.10 in the fourth quarter of 2014 and $0.10 in the first quarter of 2015, to be paid in December 2014 and March 2015, respectively. The first payment, totaling $87&nbsp;million, was executed in December 2014 and January 2015. The second payment,
totalling $87&nbsp;million, was executed in March and April 2015. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Annual General Meeting of Shareholders held on June&nbsp;13, 2014 authorized the
distribution of a semi-annual cash dividend per common share of $0.10 in the second quarter of 2014 and $0.10 in the third quarter of 2014, to be paid in June 2014 and September 2014, respectively. $89&nbsp;million corresponding to the first
distribution and $85&nbsp;million as part of the second distribution were paid during the first nine months of 2014. The remaining second portion of dividends to be paid of $4&nbsp;million was paid during the fourth quarter of 2014. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Extraordinary General Meeting of Shareholders held on December&nbsp;2, 2013 authorized the distribution of a semi-annual cash dividend per common share of
$0.10 in the fourth quarter of 2013 and $0.10 in the first quarter of 2014, to be paid in December 2013 and March 2014, respectively. The first payment, totaling $89&nbsp;million, was executed in December 2013. The remaining $0.10 per share cash
dividend totaling $89&nbsp;million was paid during the first half of 2014. </P> <P STYLE="font-size:18pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company receives significant public funding from governmental agencies in several jurisdictions. Public funding for
research and development is recognized ratably as the related costs are incurred once the agreement with the respective governmental agency has been signed and all applicable conditions have been met. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Phase-out</FONT> costs are costs incurred during the closing stage of a Company&#146;s manufacturing facility. They are
treated in the same manner as <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">start-up</FONT> costs. <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Start-up</FONT> costs represent costs incurred in the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">start-up</FONT> and testing of the
Company&#146;s new manufacturing facilities, before reaching the earlier of a minimum level of production or six months after the fabrication line&#146;s quality certification. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Exchange gains and losses, net represent the portion of exchange rate changes on transactions denominated in currencies other than an entity&#146;s functional
currency and the changes in fair value of trading derivative instruments which are not designated as hedge and which have a cash flow effect related to operating transactions, as described in Note 24. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Patent costs include legal and attorney fees and payment for claims, patent <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">pre-litigation</FONT> consultancy and legal fees.
They are reported net of settlements, if any, which primarily include reimbursements of prior patent litigation costs. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">In 2016, gain on sale of
businesses and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-current</FONT> assets is mostly related to the sale of a building in France. In 2015, it was related to the sale of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-strategic</FONT> assets, while in 2014, it
was mainly related to the sale of the Smart Connectivity Business (Display Port products). </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">F-41 </P>

<TD VALIGN="bottom"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD>&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD>&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD>&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; border-top:3.00px double #000000">&nbsp;</P></TD>
<TD>&nbsp;</TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B><I>Impairment charges </I></B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">In 2016, the Company recorded impairment charges of $8&nbsp;million, primarily consisting of $3&nbsp;million relating to the reclassification of certain
property, plant and equipment as Assets Held for Sale and $4&nbsp;million for other acquired intangible assets for which there was no alternative future use, as detailed in Notes 10 and 9, respectively. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">In 2015, the Company recorded impairment charges of $16&nbsp;million, of which $13&nbsp;million following the annual impairment test performed in the third
quarter and $3&nbsp;million for other acquired intangible assets for which there was no alternative future use. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">In 2014, the Company recorded impairment
charges of $24&nbsp;million, of which $23&nbsp;million on the former Digital Convergence Group &#150; now included in Microcontrollers, and Digital ICs Group &#150; dedicated intangible assets and $1&nbsp;million on other intangible assets, as
detailed in Note 9. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B><I>Restructuring charges and other related closure costs </I></B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company was engaged in 2016 in one major restructuring plan, the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Set-top</FONT> Box plan. In 2016, the Company announced
its decision to cease the development of new platforms and standard products for <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">set-top-box</FONT></FONT> and home gateway products. This decision implied a global workforce review
that may affect approximately 1,400 employees worldwide, which includes about 430 in France through a voluntary departure plan, about 670 in Asia and about 120 in the United States of America. The Company recorded $83&nbsp;million of restructuring
charges for this plan in 2016, of which $9&nbsp;million related to contracts that will continue with no future economic benefits to the Company and $74&nbsp;million related to employee ongoing termination benefits, primarily for employee termination
benefits in France, in the United States and Asia. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">In 2014, the Company committed to a plan affecting around 450 employees worldwide and targeting
savings in the former Embedded Processing Solutions business (the &#147;EPS restructuring plan&#148;). The Company recorded $1&nbsp;million of restructuring charges for this plan in 2016. In 2013, the Company committed to restructuring
</P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">F-42 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>

actions to reduce operating expenses, net of R&amp;D grants to the level of $600 to $650&nbsp;million on a quarterly basis (the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">&#147;$600-650&nbsp;million</FONT>
net opex plan&#148;). The Company recorded $1&nbsp;million of restructuring charges for this plan in 2016. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">In July 2013, the Company announced that it
would wind down certain 6-inch manufacturing lines, close its back-end plant in Longgang and consolidate back-end activities in China to Shenzhen (the &#147;Manufacturing consolidation plan&#148;). There was no charge recorded by the Company for
this plan in 2016. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">In 2015, the Company incurred restructuring charges and other related closure costs for $49&nbsp;million corresponding to: </P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">An amount of $49&nbsp;million is expected to be paid within twelve months, as detailed in Note 13. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Set-top</FONT> Box restructuring plan is expected to result in a total charge of $170&nbsp;million, of which
$83&nbsp;million was recorded during the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2016. The plan will be substantially completed in 2017 in all locations except France. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">$600-650&nbsp;million</FONT> net opex plan resulted in a total charge of $115&nbsp;million. The plan was substantially
completed in 2014. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Manufacturing consolidation plan resulted in a total charge of $31&nbsp;million, excluding impairments. The plan was completed in
2015. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The EPS restructuring plan resulted in a total charge of $67&nbsp;million. The plan was substantially completed in 2015. </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">F-43 </P>

actual costs that the Company will incur may differ from these estimates based on the timing required to complete the restructuring plan, the number of people involved, the final agreed termination benefits and the costs associated with the transfer
of equipment, products and processes. </P> <P STYLE="font-size:18pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The tax holidays represent a tax exemption period aimed to attract foreign technological investment in certain tax
jurisdictions. The effect of the tax benefits, from tax holidays for countries which are profitable, on basic earnings per share was $0.05, $0.05 and $0.07 for the years ended December&nbsp;31, 2016, 2015, and 2014, respectively. These agreements
are present in various countries and include programs that reduce up to and including 100% of taxes in years affected by the agreements. The Company&#146;s tax holidays expire at various dates through the year ending December&nbsp;31, 2022. In
certain countries, tax holidays can be renewed depending on the Company still meeting certain conditions at the date of expiration of the current tax holidays. </P>

<TD VALIGN="top"> <P STYLE=" margin-top:0pt ; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:1.00em; text-indent:-1.00em; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>Net deferred income tax asset</B></P></TD>

<TD>&nbsp;</TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">For a particular <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">tax-paying</FONT> component of the Company and within a particular tax
jurisdiction, all current deferred tax liabilities and assets are offset and presented as a single amount, similarly to <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-current</FONT> deferred tax liabilities and assets. The Company does not offset deferred tax
liabilities and assets attributable to different <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">tax-paying</FONT> components or to different tax jurisdictions. </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">F-45 </P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The valuation allowance for a particular tax jurisdiction is allocated between current and
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-current</FONT> deferred tax assets for that jurisdiction on a pro rata basis. The &#147;Tax credits granted on past capital investments&#148; mainly related to a 2003 agreement granting the Company certain tax
credits for capital investments purchased through the year ending December&nbsp;31, 2006. Any unused tax credits granted under the agreement will be impacted by yearly by a legal inflationary index (currently
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">-0.04%</FONT> per annum). The credits may be utilized through 2020 or later depending on the Company meeting certain program criteria. In addition to this agreement, starting in 2007 the Company continues to receive
tax credits on the yearly capital investments, which may be used to offset that year&#146;s tax liabilities and increases by the legal inflationary rate. However, pursuant to the inability to utilize these credits currently and in future years, the
Company did not recognize any deferred tax asset on such tax allowance. As a result, there is no financial impact to the net deferred tax assets of the Company. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The amounts of deferred tax benefit (expense) recorded as a component of other comprehensive income (loss) was $(1) million and $(3) million in 2016 and 2015,
respectively. They were related primarily to the tax effects of the recognized unfunded status on defined benefits plan. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The cumulative amount of
distributable earnings related to the Company&#146;s investments in foreign subsidiaries and corporate joint ventures was $785 million and $626&nbsp;million as at December&nbsp;31, 2016 and December&nbsp;31, 2015, respectively. Due to the
Company&#146;s legal and tax structure, with the parent company established in the Netherlands, there was no significant tax impact from the distribution of earnings from investments in foreign subsidiaries and corporate joint ventures. This is
because there is no tax impact on dividends paid up to a Dutch holding company. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">A reconciliation of 2016, 2015 and 2014 beginning and ending amounts of

classified as a reduction of deferred tax assets. It is reasonably possible that certain of the uncertain tax positions disclosed in the table above could increase within the next 12 months due to ongoing tax audits. The Company is not able to make
an estimate of the range of the reasonably possible change. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Additionally, the Company elected to classify accrued interest and penalties related to
uncertain tax positions as components of income tax expense in the consolidated statements of income, they were $1&nbsp;million in 2016, </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">F-46 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">
$1&nbsp;million in 2015, $27&nbsp;million in 2014 and not material in the previous years. At December&nbsp;31, 2016 and 2015, accrued interest and penalties amounted to $9&nbsp;million. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The tax years that remain open for review in the Company&#146;s major tax jurisdictions, including France, Italy, United States and India, are from 1996 to
2016. </P> <P STYLE="font-size:18pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>

<TD>&nbsp;</TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Operating leases are mainly related to building and equipment leases. The amount disclosed is composed of minimum payments for
future leases from 2017 to 2021 and thereafter. The Company leases land, buildings, plants and equipment under operating leases that expire at various dates under <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-cancellable</FONT> lease agreements. Operating
lease expense was $56&nbsp;million for both the years ended December&nbsp;31, 2016 and 2015 and $66&nbsp;million for the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2014. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Purchase obligations are primarily comprised of purchase commitments for equipment, for outsourced foundry wafers and for software licenses. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Other obligations primarily relate to firm contractual commitments with respect to partnership and cooperation agreements and other service agreements. </P>
<P STYLE="font-size:18pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE; font-family:Times New Roman; font-size:10pt" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR style = "page-break-inside:avoid">
<TD WIDTH="4%">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD WIDTH="4%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><B>23.</B></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"><B>CONTINGENCIES, CLAIMS AND LEGAL PROCEEDINGS </B></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company is subject to possible loss contingencies
arising in the ordinary course of business. These include but are not limited to: warranty cost on the products of the Company, breach of contract claims, claims for unauthorized use of third-party intellectual property, tax claims beyond assessed
uncertain tax positions as well as claims for environmental damages. In determining loss contingencies, the Company considers the likelihood of impairing an asset or the incurrence of a liability at the date of the consolidated financial statements
as well as the ability to reasonably estimate the amount of such loss. The Company records a provision for a loss contingency when information available before the consolidated financial statements are issued or are available to be issued indicates
that it is probable that an asset has been impaired or a liability has been incurred at the date of the consolidated financial statements and when the amount of loss can be reasonably estimated. The Company regularly
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">re-evaluates</FONT> claims to determine whether provisions need to be readjusted based on the most current information available to the Company. Changes in these evaluations could result in an adverse material impact
on the Company&#146;s results of operations, cash flows or its financial position for the period in which they occur. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company has received and may in
the future receive communications alleging possible infringements of third party patents or other third party intellectual property rights. Furthermore, the Company from time to time enters into discussions regarding a broad patent cross license
arrangement with other industry participants. There is no assurance that such discussions may be brought to a successful conclusion and result in the intended agreement. The Company may become involved in costly litigation brought against the
Company regarding patents, mask works, copyrights, trademarks or trade secrets. In the event that the outcome of any litigation would be unfavorable to the Company, the Company may be required to take a license to third party patents and/or other
intellectual property rights at economically unfavorable terms and conditions, and possibly pay damages for prior use and/or face an injunction, all of which individually or in the aggregate could have a material adverse effect on the Company&#146;s
results of operations, cash flows, financial position and/or ability to compete. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company is otherwise also involved in various lawsuits, claims,
investigations and proceedings incidental to its business and operations. </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">F-47 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company regularly evaluates claims and legal proceedings together with their related probable losses to
determine whether they need to be adjusted based on the current information available to the Company. There can be no assurance that its recorded reserves will be sufficient to cover the extent of its potential liabilities. Legal costs associated
with claims are expensed as incurred. In the event of litigation which is adversely determined with respect to the Company&#146;s interests, or in the event the Company needs to change its evaluation of a potential third-party claim, based on new
evidence or communications, a material adverse effect could impact its operations or financial condition at the time it were to materialize. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">As of
December&nbsp;31, 2016, provisions for estimated probable losses with respect to claims and legal proceedings were not considered material. </P> <P STYLE="font-size:18pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE; font-family:Times New Roman; font-size:10pt" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR style = "page-break-inside:avoid">
<TD WIDTH="4%">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD WIDTH="4%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><B>24.</B></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"><B>FINANCIAL INSTRUMENTS AND RISK MANAGEMENT </B></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:6%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B>24.1 Financial risk factors </B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company is exposed to changes in financial market conditions in the normal course of business due to its operations in different foreign currencies and its
ongoing investing and financing activities. The Company&#146;s activities expose it to a variety of financial risks: market risk (including foreign exchange risk, fair value interest rate risk, cash flow interest rate risk and price risk), credit
risk and liquidity risk. The Company&#146;s overall risk management program focuses on the unpredictability of financial markets and seeks to minimize potential adverse effects on the Company&#146;s financial performance. The Company uses derivative
financial instruments to hedge certain risk exposures. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Financial risk management is carried out by a central treasury department (Corporate Treasury).
Additionally, a Treasury Committee, chaired by the CFO, steers treasury activities and ensures compliance with corporate policies. Treasury activities are thus regulated by the Company&#146;s policies, which define procedures, objectives and
controls. The policies focus on the management of financial risk in terms of exposure to market risk, credit risk and liquidity risk. Treasury controls are subject to internal audits. Most treasury activities are centralized, with any local treasury
activities subject to oversight from Corporate Treasury. Corporate Treasury identifies, evaluates and hedges financial risks in close cooperation with the Company&#146;s operating units. It provides written principles for overall risk management, as
well as written policies covering specific areas, such as foreign exchange risk, interest rate risk, price risk, credit risk, use of derivative financial instruments, and investments of excess liquidity. The majority of cash and cash equivalents is
held in U.S. dollars and Euros and is placed with financial institutions rated at least a single &#147;A&#148; long-term rating from two of the major rating agencies, meaning at least A3 from Moody&#146;s Investor Service and <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">A-</FONT> from Standard&nbsp;&amp; Poor&#146;s and Fitch Ratings, or better. These ratings are closely and continuously monitored in order to manage exposure to the counterparty&#146;s risk. Hedging transactions are
performed only to hedge exposures deriving from operating, investing and financing activities conducted in the normal course of business. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><B><I>Market risk </I></B></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><I>Foreign exchange risk </I></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company
conducts its business on a global basis in various major international currencies. As a result, the Company is exposed to adverse movements in foreign currency exchange rates, primarily with respect to the Euro. Foreign exchange risk mainly arises
from recognized assets and liabilities at the Company&#146;s subsidiaries and future commercial transactions. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Management has set up a policy to require
the Company&#146;s subsidiaries to hedge their entire foreign exchange risk exposure with the Company through financial instruments transacted or overseen by Corporate Treasury. To manage their foreign exchange risk arising from
foreign-currency-denominated assets and liabilities, subsidiaries use forward contracts and purchased currency options. Foreign exchange risk arises when recognized assets and liabilities are denominated in a currency that is not the entity&#146;s
functional currency. These instruments do not qualify as hedging instruments for accounting purposes. Forward contracts and currency options, including collars, are also used by the Company to reduce its exposure to U.S. dollar fluctuations in
Euro-denominated forecasted intercompany transactions that cover a large part of its research and development, selling, general and administrative expenses as well as a portion of its <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">front-end</FONT> manufacturing
costs of semi-finished goods. The Company also hedges through the use of currency forward contracts certain Singapore dollar-denominated manufacturing forecasted transactions. The derivative instruments used to hedge these forecasted transactions
meet the criteria for designation as cash flow hedge. The hedged forecasted transactions have a high probability of occurring for hedge accounting purposes. </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">F-48 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">It is the Company&#146;s policy to have the foreign exchange exposures in all the currencies hedged month by
month against the monthly standard rate. At each month end, the forecasted flows for the coming month are hedged together with the fixing of the new standard rate. For this reason the hedging transactions will have an exchange rate very close to the
standard rate at which the forecasted flows will be recorded on the following month. As such, the foreign exchange exposure of the Company, which consists in the balance sheet positions and other contractually agreed transactions, is always close to
zero and any movement in the foreign exchange rates will not therefore influence the exchange effect on items of the consolidated statement of income. Any discrepancy from the forecasted values and the actual results is constantly monitored and
prompt actions are taken, if needed. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><I>Derivative Instruments Not Designated as a Hedge </I></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company enters into foreign currency forward contracts to reduce its exposure to changes in exchange rates and the associated risk arising from the
denomination of certain assets and liabilities in foreign currencies in the Company&#146;s subsidiaries. These include receivables from international sales by various subsidiaries, payables for foreign currency-denominated purchases and certain
other assets and liabilities arising from intercompany transactions. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The notional amount of these financial instruments totaled $375&nbsp;million,
$372&nbsp;million and $286&nbsp;million at December&nbsp;31, 2016, 2015 and 2014, respectively. The principal currencies covered at the end of the year 2016 are the Euro, the Japanese yen, the Singapore dollar, the Indian rupee, the Swiss franc, the
China Yuan Renminbi, the British pound and the South Korean won. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The risk of loss associated with forward contracts is equal to the exchange rate
differential from the time the contract is entered into until the time it is settled. The risk of loss associated with purchased currency options is equal to the premium paid when the option is not exercised. </P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Foreign currency forward contracts not designated as cash flow hedge outstanding as of December&nbsp;31, 2016 have remaining terms of 3 days to 254 days,
maturing on average after 26 days. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><I>Derivative Instruments Designated as a Hedge </I></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">To further reduce its exposure to U.S. dollar exchange rate fluctuations, the Company hedges through the use of currency forward contracts and currency
options, including collars, certain Euro-denominated forecasted intercompany transactions that cover at <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">year-end</FONT> a large part of its research and development, selling, general and administrative expenses, as
well as a portion of its <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">front-end</FONT> manufacturing costs of semi-finished goods within cost of sales. The Company also hedges through the use of currency forward contracts certain manufacturing transactions
within cost of sales denominated in Singapore dollars. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The principles regulating the hedging strategy for derivatives designated as cash flow hedge are
established as follows: (i)&nbsp;for R&amp;D and corporate costs, up to 80% of the total forecasted transactions; (ii)&nbsp;for manufacturing costs, up to 70% of the total forecasted transactions. In order to follow a dynamic hedge strategy, the
Company may change the percentage of the designated hedged item within the limit of 100% of the forecasted transaction. The maximum length of time over which the Company could hedge its exposure to the variability of cash flows for forecasted
transactions is 24 months. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">For the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2016, the Company recorded an increase in cost of sales of $7&nbsp;million and an
increase in operating expenses of $2&nbsp;million, related to the realized losses incurred on such hedged transactions. For the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2015, the Company recorded an increase in cost of sales of $105&nbsp;million and an increase
in operating expenses of $65&nbsp;million, related to the realized losses incurred on such hedged transactions. For the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2014, the Company recorded an increase in cost of sales of $1&nbsp;million and an increase in
operating expenses of $1&nbsp;million, related to the realized losses incurred on such hedged transactions. No significant ineffective portion of the hedge was recorded on the line &#147;Other income and expenses, net&#148; of the consolidated
statements of income for the years ended December&nbsp;31, 2016, 2015 and 2014. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The notional amount of foreign currency forward contracts and currency
options, including collars, designated as cash flow hedge totaled $1,370&nbsp;million, $1,449&nbsp;million and $1,386&nbsp;million at December&nbsp;31, 2016, 2015 and 2014, respectively. The forecasted transactions hedged at December&nbsp;31, 2016
were determined to have a high probability of occurring. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">As of December&nbsp;31, 2016, $47&nbsp;million of deferred losses on derivative instruments
included in &#147;Accumulated other comprehensive income (loss)&#148; were expected to be reclassified as earnings during the next 12 months based </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">F-49 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

on the monthly forecasted research and development expenses, corporate costs and semi-finished manufacturing costs. No amount was reclassified as &#147;Other income and expenses, net&#148; into
the consolidated statement of income from &#147;Accumulated other comprehensive income (loss)&#148; in the consolidated statement of equity. Foreign currency forward contracts, currency options and collars designated as cash flow hedge outstanding
as of December&nbsp;31, 2016 have remaining terms of 4 days to 19 months, maturing on average after 153 days. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">As at December&nbsp;31, 2016, the Company
had the following outstanding derivative instruments that were entered into to hedge Euro-denominated and Singapore dollar-denominated forecasted transactions: </P> <P STYLE="font-size:12pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>

<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom">&nbsp;</TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><I>Cash flow and fair value interest rate risk </I></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company&#146;s interest rate risk arises from long-term borrowings. Borrowings issued at variable rates expose the Company to cash flow interest rate risk.
Borrowings issued at fixed rates expose the Company to fair value interest rate risk. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">The Company analyses its interest rate exposure on a dynamic basis.
Various scenarios are simulated taking into consideration refinancing, renewal of existing positions, alternative financing and hedging. The Company invests primarily on a short-term basis and as such the Company&#146;s liquidity is invested in
floating interest rate instruments. As a consequence the Company is exposed to interest rate risk due to potential mismatch between the return on its short term floating interest rate investments and the portion of its long term debt issued at fixed
rate. </P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18pt; margin-bottom:0pt; margin-left:4%; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman"><I>Price risk </I></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">As part of its
ongoing investing activities, the Company may be exposed to equity security price risk for investments in public entities. In order to hedge the exposure to this market risk, the Company may enter into certain derivative hedging transactions. </P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt ; font-size:8pt">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman" ALIGN="center">F-50 </P>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0pt; margin-bottom:0pt; font-size:10pt; font-family:Times New Roman">Information on fair value of derivative instruments and their location in the consolidated balance sheets as at
December&nbsp;31, 2016 and December&nbsp;31, 2015 is presented in the table below: </P> <P STYLE="font-size:12pt;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt">&nbsp;</P>
